metadata.title: Timothy Okooboh | Senior Frontend Engineer
metadata.description: Senior frontend engineer with 7+ years building Vue.js and React.js products, with strengths in design systems, frontend architecture, accessibility, and performance.

navigation.mobileDescription: Browse site sections and quick actions.

hero.description: Senior frontend engineer with 7+ years of professional experience building Vue.js and React.js applications, especially around design systems, frontend architecture, accessibility, and performance. I’m deliberately strengthening the design side of my practice and documenting that progress alongside the engineering work.
hero.availability: Open to senior frontend roles, design systems work, and selective contracts.

positioning.title: Current focus at SeamlessHR.
positioning.description: At SeamlessHR, I lead the UI library team and maintain the shared design system across components, documentation, tooling, and patterns. I work closely with designers and engineers so accessibility is built into the product foundation.

principles.title: Depth, range, flexibility, and agency.
principles.description: The through-line in my work is not just shipping interfaces. It is taking ownership of systems, understanding product constraints deeply, and making deliberate technical decisions that improve the experience for both users and teams.

depth.title: Depth in systems
depth.description: I like working below the surface: component contracts, accessibility rules, documentation patterns, and the architecture that keeps product work from drifting.

range.title: Range across contexts
range.description: My work spans internal tooling, open source, AI workflows, design systems, commerce, and product features with very different constraints and audiences.

flexibility.title: Flexible execution
flexibility.description: I can move from polished UI work to deeper engineering problems, from leading shared foundations to shipping contract work under tight timelines.

agency.title: Agency and ownership
agency.description: I do my best work where there is room to think, propose better approaches, and carry execution responsibly from idea to implementation.

seamlesshr.note: Core maintainer of SeamlessHR's Vue.js design system component library, leading work across accessibility, frontend architecture, documentation, and implementation quality.
highlights: Led the development of 40+ reusable and accessible Vue.js design system components, driving consistency and scalability across the company’s products.
Developed analytics dashboards for tracking employee performance metrics, using Chart.js for data visualisation and real-time monitoring of team objectives and key results.
Implemented an automated performance improvement plan workflow that tracks employee performance reviews and recommends improvement plans based on team objectives and key results.
Defined and monitored Core Web Vitals targets across products, using Lighthouse to set measurable frontend performance baselines.

olanna.note: Ownership across real-time AI chat and voice experiences, authentication flows, and knowledge-base management tooling.
highlights: Integrated secure authentication flows including magic links, OTP, 2FA, OAuth, password reset, and phone login.
Shipped real-time conversation experiences for chat, voice previews, inbox flows, and listen-in sessions using WebSockets.
Proposed the shared-schema generative UI contract that the team adopted for agent-driven UI rendering.

realtrac.note: Contract work across frontend architecture, analytics dashboards, and payment flows for a real-estate platform.
highlights: Built scalable frontend architecture for a real-estate platform serving global property purchases.
Developed analytics dashboards that made platform activity easier for operators to track.
Integrated secure payment flows for international property transactions.

independent.note: A mix of solo client execution and self-initiated frontend systems work that sharpens product judgment and API design taste.
highlights: Built MyFastMeds end to end across Next.js and Nest.js, owning both the storefront UX and backend workflows as a solo engineer.
Created VBox, a typed style-props engine for Vue that supports theming, aliases, responsive tokens, and polymorphic composition.
Use independent projects to test abstractions, deepen systems thinking, and turn curiosity into concrete product-quality execution.

lixy.note: Frontend work on a Nuxt SSR application with a strong emphasis on performance and product utility.
highlights: Built and optimized a Nuxt SSR application, improving TTFB and LCP through code-splitting and lazy loading.
Developed a custom chat widget that became part of the company’s launch strategy and improved customer engagement.
Migrated map functionality from Google Maps to Leaflet.js, significantly reducing API costs.

flux.note: Product work focused on improving the buying experience and overall performance of a food delivery platform.
highlights: Improved UX quality across the product experience.
Improved checkout performance for a food delivery platform.

andela.note: Early professional training period where I built frontend applications against real APIs and product requirements.
highlights: Built frontend applications with HTML, CSS, JavaScript, and REST APIs.
This was the start of the professional path I have been building on since 2019.

hero.title: Frontend work with real product depth.
hero.description: A focused set of projects across design systems, AI workflows, commerce, and open source. The common thread is ownership: understanding the product, shaping the system, and shipping interfaces that hold up in real use.

professional.title: Case studies from shipped product work.
professional.description: These projects show how I work inside real product constraints: building reusable systems, partnering closely with design, and carrying frontend execution from architecture through shipped detail.

lab.title: Open source work that sharpened my frontend instincts.
lab.description: VBox started as an experiment, but it still reflects how I like to work: strong primitives, ergonomic APIs, and abstractions that stay useful in real product code.

cta.title: Need a senior frontend engineer who can own the system behind the interface?
cta.description: That usually means shared libraries, dense product flows, or frontend architecture work where accessibility, maintainability, and implementation quality all need real attention.

hero.title: Seamkit UI: raising the accessibility and consistency bar across SeamlessHR products.
hero.description: SeamlessHR's shared design system and Storybook documentation surface, built to improve accessibility, reduce development time across teams, and give product teams a more reliable frontend foundation.

metrics: 50%+ reduction in development time across teams
overview: At SeamlessHR, I lead the UI library team and maintain Seamkit UI, the shared component library and documentation surface used across the company’s products. The work is not just about shipping components. It is about giving teams a frontend foundation they can trust and scale with.

I defined the principles behind the system, created the documentation, led engineers building the library, and worked closely with designers on the UX details of each component. I also organised accessibility sessions for frontend engineers because better primitives alone were not enough to improve product quality.

challenge.title: The challenge
challenge.description: Before Seamkit UI, accessibility and consistency were weak across product teams. Keyboard support was unreliable, shared patterns were under-defined, and the previous component library did not treat accessibility as a first-class concern.

points.title: Accessibility had no strong foundation
points.description: Teams were composing interfaces on top of primitives that did not adequately support semantics, focus management, or keyboard interaction.

points.title: System adoption needed more than components
points.description: Even with better primitives, developers still needed documentation, composition guidance, and education to build accessible product surfaces well.

implementation.title: What I built
implementation.description: I led the architecture and delivery of the library, built many of the components myself, and structured the Storybook docs around foundations, accessibility guidance, and composition patterns teams could apply directly in product code.
implementation.galleryCaption: A documentation surface that makes the system teachable, usable, and easier to trust.

landing.title: System documentation landing
landing.caption: The welcome surface introduces foundations, accessibility standards, and component guidance as one coherent product, not a loose set of docs.
landing.alt: Seamkit UI Storybook welcome page showing documentation navigation and design system overview

components-overview.title: Component catalogue overview
components-overview.caption: The catalogue turns a large surface area into something teams can scan quickly, with each family mapped to a clear implementation page.
components-overview.alt: Seamkit UI components overview page listing component families like Accordion, Alert, Avatar, Badge, and Banner

accessibility.title: Accessibility guidance surface
accessibility.caption: Accessibility expectations live inside the system docs themselves, with concrete references for keyboard flow, focus treatment, ARIA, and contrast.
accessibility.alt: Seamkit UI accessibility documentation page showing composition review guidance and accessibility checks

foundations.title: Token foundations reference
foundations.caption: Foundations documentation translates raw token decisions into practical references engineers and designers can inspect while building real interfaces.
foundations.alt: Seamkit UI foundations page documenting color tokens and system foundations

implementation.reflection: Seamkit UI taught me that design-system work succeeds when component quality, documentation, and team habits improve together. Shipping primitives is only part of the job.

hero.title: Olanna: frontend systems for real-time AI chat and voice workflows.
hero.description: A three-month contract where I owned frontend work across authentication, knowledge-base tooling, chat and voice product surfaces, and the schema-driven contract behind generative UI responses.

metrics: Magic link, 2FA, OAuth, phone, and password auth flows
Real-time voice and chat conversation surfaces
Schema-driven generative UI proposal adopted by the team
overview: Olanna provisions AI agents for businesses and connects those agents to channels like phone calls, messaging, and WhatsApp. During my contract, I worked on the frontend systems that let business owners configure agents, simulate conversations, manage knowledge, and monitor interactions in real time.

I also contributed to the UI library foundations by fixing accessibility issues in existing components and building new primitives like pagination and multiselect. That foundation work made it easier to move quickly on the more complex conversational features.

challenge.title: The challenge
challenge.description: Olanna combined real-time conversations, multiple authentication flows, agent configuration, and knowledge-base management in one product. The challenge was making those flows feel usable without exposing all of the underlying system complexity to business users.

points.title: Real-time UX had to feel dependable
points.description: Chat, voice, listen-in sessions, and live transcriptions all depended on WebSockets and responsive state handling.

points.title: Generative UI needed a cleaner contract
points.description: The initial proposal put too much templating responsibility on the backend. I proposed a shared schema model so the frontend could safely map agent responses to real UI components.

implementation.title: What I built
implementation.description: I built the authentication suite, reusable conversation hooks and primitives, super-agent interactions, agent preview settings, listen-in experiences, inbox flows, and the knowledge-base UX feeding the RAG architecture. I also wrote the generative UI proposal the team used as the implementation path.
implementation.galleryCaption: A set of product surfaces that made AI conversations, settings, and knowledge operations feel more concrete to business users.

agent-home.title: Agent query surface
agent-home.caption: A calm starting point for asking the provisioned agent business questions through chat or voice.
agent-home.alt: Olanna agent home view with a large conversation input for querying the agent

personality-preview.title: Settings with live preview
personality-preview.caption: Agent personality, language, and preview modes sit side by side so configuration decisions stay grounded in immediate feedback.
personality-preview.alt: Olanna agent settings view showing personality controls beside a live agent preview

voice-settings.title: Voice tuning controls
voice-settings.caption: Tone, speed, and depth controls are grouped with a live voice preview so operators can tune behavior with confidence.
voice-settings.alt: Olanna voice settings screen with sliders for tone, personality, speed, and depth

model-settings.title: Model selection controls
model-settings.caption: Provider and model choices are exposed clearly so backend intelligence decisions feel understandable from the product surface.
model-settings.alt: Olanna model settings screen listing language model provider and model choices

inbox-thread.title: Inbox conversation view
inbox-thread.caption: The split layout keeps the customer thread readable while preserving contact context and message history.
inbox-thread.alt: Olanna inbox layout showing a conversation list beside an open message thread

live-call.title: Real-time listen-in
live-call.caption: Business owners can join live calls, follow transcript updates, and stay close to active customer interactions.
live-call.alt: Olanna live call listen-in view with participants and real-time transcript updates

call-summary.title: Generated call summary
call-summary.caption: Summaries convert live transcript detail into a quick operational snapshot that teams can scan after the call.
call-summary.alt: Olanna call summary card generated from an ongoing voice conversation

implementation.reflection: Olanna reminded me that strong frontend work is not only about what gets rendered. One of my most valuable contributions was helping the team settle on a cleaner generative UI contract.

hero.title: MyFastMeds: solo end-to-end delivery for a medicine commerce platform.
hero.description: A solo contract build where I owned both the frontend and backend of a medicine marketplace for healthcare and non-healthcare professionals.

metrics: Frontend and backend ownership by one engineer
Storefront, cart, checkout, and admin workflows built
overview: MyFastMeds is a health-commerce product for purchasing medicines with delivery support. Because I was the only engineer on the project, I owned the full path from UI decisions to API design and the backend workflows supporting the product.

That kind of setup changes the work. I was not only implementing screens, but also deciding how search, cart, checkout, account flows, inventory behavior, and operational rules should fit together across the whole system.

challenge.title: The challenge
challenge.description: The product needed to feel trustworthy and straightforward in a sensitive domain, while still moving fast enough for a client engagement being built by one engineer.

points.title: Trust had to be visible in the UX
points.description: Because the domain is medicine procurement, the interface needed to communicate reliability, product availability, and purchasing clarity from the first screen.

points.title: Solo ownership meant every decision connected
points.description: Frontend states, backend contracts, cart rules, and checkout behavior all had to be designed and implemented with the same mental model.

implementation.title: What I built
implementation.description: I built the storefront, category navigation, product pages, cart, and checkout flows, and I designed the backend APIs and business logic required to support them. The work involved constant trade-offs between speed, clarity, and long-term coherence.
implementation.galleryCaption: A still-private product foundation that shows end-to-end ownership across interface design, application architecture, and backend workflows.

storefront-home.title: Marketplace landing
storefront-home.caption: The landing surface makes trust, search, and product discovery visible immediately for buyers navigating a health-commerce experience.
storefront-home.alt: MyFastMeds landing page showing search, marketplace hero, and product categories

catalog.title: Category-led product discovery
catalog.caption: The catalogue balances categories, pricing, and add-to-cart actions so buyers can browse inventory quickly without losing trust cues.
catalog.alt: MyFastMeds catalogue page showing new arrivals and popular product listings

notifications.title: Order notifications workflow
notifications.caption: The notifications surface keeps unread state, order updates, and bulk actions visible so operational follow-up feels lightweight.
notifications.alt: MyFastMeds notifications page showing read and unread order alerts

cart.title: Cart and checkout handoff
cart.caption: Quantity controls, stock visibility, totals, and the order summary stay on one screen so buyers can validate decisions before moving into payment.
cart.alt: MyFastMeds cart page showing item quantities, totals, and checkout summary

implementation.reflection: MyFastMeds strengthened my ability to think across the whole product, not just the UI. When one engineer owns both sides of the system, clear architecture becomes a product decision, not just a technical one.

hero.title: VBox: bringing typed style props and polymorphism to Vue.
hero.description: An open source experiment inspired by Chakra UI’s style props, built to give Vue developers a typed, polymorphic styling primitive with theming, aliases, and responsive support.

metrics: Typed support for every CSS property as props
Used to style the VueDrill landing page end to end
overview: VBox started from a simple observation: I liked the style-props experience in Chakra UI, but I did not see a Vue alternative that felt equally ergonomic.
I built it as a polymorphic component plus lightweight styling engine with type-safe style props, responsive values, pseudo states, vendor prefixing, and design-token customization.

challenge.title: The challenge
challenge.description: The hard part was designing an API that stayed expressive without becoming unreadable, while still giving developers reliable autocomplete, theming hooks, and room for custom aliases.

points.title: Ergonomics had to justify the abstraction
points.description: If the API felt noisy or unpredictable, the whole experiment would lose its value for real application work.

points.title: System behavior needed to stay flexible
points.description: Responsive breakpoints, theme values, pseudo states, and aliasing all had to coexist without turning the API into a type-safety mess.

implementation.title: What I built
implementation.description: I designed the component API, implemented the styling engine and type system, and added support for themes, aliases, responsive values, pseudo states, and vendor prefixing. I also used VBox on a real landing page to make sure the abstraction held up outside a demo.
implementation.galleryCaption: A focused open source experiment that reflects how I think about APIs, developer experience, and reusable frontend primitives.

marketing-home.title: Developer-facing landing page
marketing-home.caption: The homepage demonstrates the abstraction quickly: typed style props, theming, and lightweight ergonomics framed for Vue developers evaluating the idea.
marketing-home.alt: VBox landing page introducing the polymorphic Vue component and style props system

style-props.title: Docs with live API examples
style-props.caption: The documentation pairs the style-props API with rendered output so Vue developers can understand the abstraction without guessing how it behaves.
style-props.alt: VBox documentation page showing the style props API and rendered example output

vbox-config.title: Configuration and type setup
vbox-config.caption: The configuration docs explain how aliases, breakpoints, themes, and generated types fit together in a real project setup.
vbox-config.alt: VBox documentation page explaining VBox configuration and generated type support

implementation.reflection: VBox reminded me that experiments become more useful when you push them far enough to reveal real API and developer-experience trade-offs.

intro.title: Why this work is here now
intro.description: I do not want the design side of my practice to live as a private ambition. These studies document the work in progress: what I am testing, what I am learning, and how that learning feeds back into the way I build products.
